justification: All pass criteria met: Scenario A correctly identifies no circuit breaker leading to sustained degradation with specific fix (add circuit breaker + fallback). Scenario B includes connection pool math (20 workers / 10 connections = contention) and predicts cascading failures. Scenario C explicitly identifies that 8s responses within 10s timeout means circuit breaker will NOT trip — called out as 'the most dangerous scenario.' Scenario D correctly assessed as the best-handled case where circuit breaker 'earns its keep.' Specific improvements provided for each failing scenario. Verdict is NOT READY, correctly identifying A, B, C as unhandled.

observations.text: Scenario C analysis is exceptional — explicitly states 'circuit breaker will NOT trip' because 8s is within 10s timeout, calculates throughput drop to ~2 payments/second, and recommends latency-based circuit breaker
Scenario B goes beyond the rubric by identifying the charge-without-record data consistency risk when Stripe succeeds but DB write fails
Scenario D correctly differentiated as partially handled — acknowledges circuit breaker works but notes the initial 50s bleed window
Scenario A correctly characterized as sustained degradation (not crash), with precise prediction of 100% payment failure for full 5 minutes
Provides concrete throughput math: 20 workers / 8s per Stripe call = ~2 payments/second in Scenario C
Escalations are well-reasoned — recommends fixing known gaps before chaos testing rather than discovering them through chaos

trials.justification: All pass criteria met: Scenario A correctly identifies no circuit breaker causing sustained 2s timeout degradation with specific fix (add circuit breaker + fallback). Scenario B includes connection pool math (10 connections for 20 workers = contention) and predicts failures plus ghost charges. Scenario C explicitly identifies that slow-but-successful calls won't trip the circuit breaker — 'The circuit breaker is useless here because there are no failures' — the key resilience gap. Scenario D correctly assesses circuit breaker as effective, with detailed state-machine walkthrough. Specific improvements provided for every scenario. Verdict is NOT READY, covering A, B, and C as failing scenarios while acknowledging D is handled.

observations.text: Scenario C analysis is exceptional — clearly states 'The circuit breaker does NOT trip because requests succeed — they're just slow' and recommends latency-based circuit breaker
Scenario B goes beyond the rubric by identifying the ghost charge risk (Stripe charged but DB write fails), not just the connection contention
Scenario D includes detailed timeline analysis (50s warmup, circuit open at 110s, half-open probe fails, total ~170s impact) showing deep understanding of circuit breaker state machines
Thread pool math is precise throughout: 20 workers / 2s = 10 req/s failure throughput (A), 20 workers / 8s = 2.5 req/s (C)
Correctly differentiates severity — Scenario D is 'SERVICE SURVIVES' with circuit breaker doing its job, while A/B/C have progressively worse unhandled failure modes
Scenario D rated as 'partial' handling rather than acknowledging the circuit breaker works well — the narrative correctly describes it working but the status label understates effectiveness
Observability review adds significant value beyond the chaos scenarios, identifying that existing metrics are insufficient to even diagnose any of the four failure modes

trials.justification: All six pass criteria met. Scenario A: correctly identifies no circuit breaker causes 100% payment failure for 5 minutes with specific fix (circuit breaker + fail-open for low-value). Scenario B: explicit connection pool math (20 workers / 10 connections = 2:1 contention), predicts total service freeze and burst of errors. Scenario C: explicitly identifies that slow-but-successful calls won't trip the circuit breaker ('nothing fails — the circuit breaker never trips'), calls it the insidious scenario, provides throughput math (20/8s = 2.5 req/s). Scenario D: correctly assessed as the best-handled scenario with detailed timeline showing circuit breaker activating after ~15s. Specific improvements provided per scenario. Verdict: NOT READY, with A/B/C clearly identified as unhandled failures.

observations.text: Scenario C analysis is exceptional — explicitly states 'circuit breaker never trips (requests succeed, just slowly)' which is the key insight
Scenario D includes a detailed second-by-second timeline of circuit breaker behavior, correctly identifying it as the best-handled case
Scenario B goes beyond the rubric by identifying that lost connections are permanent due to no retry, causing degradation even after recovery
Throughput math for Scenario C is precise: 20 threads / 8s = 2.5 req/s
Each scenario has concrete, differentiated mitigations rather than generic resilience advice
Observability review adds significant value by identifying what metrics are missing to detect each specific failure mode

Scenario A analysis assumes 503 returns fast (~50ms) and focuses on payment failure rate rather than the 2s timeout degradation path specified in the rubric — though it does mention the 2s timeout in timeout_config section
